P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
Stepnen C. Williams 
Attorney at Law

Walnut CreeK, CA 
94598 
Dear Mr. Williams: 
Executive/Legal 
322-5901 
June 9, 1983 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Our Advice No. A-83-146 
Thank you for your letter to the Commission regarding the advice 
given to Assemoly Speaker Wilile Brown concerning a possiDle conflict 
of interest. 
You have noted correctly that Section 87100 of the 
Government COdell applies to all public officials, and that, under 
Sections 82048 and 82049, state legislators are puolic officials. 
You also noted, again correctly, that the sanctions available against 
other public officials for violations of Section 87100 dO not apply 
to state legislators. 
Section 87102. 
Several policy conslderations underlie the general exemption of 
legls1ators and otner elected state officials from the remedies 
provided for violations of Section 87100. 
There has been a strong 
tradition in tnis country of noninterference in the legislative 
process Dy either tne Judlcial or the executive brancn of government, 
arising out of the prlnciples of separation of powers and of immunity 
of legislators with respect to their legls1ative acts. 
Both tne 
California and the United States Supreme Courts have upheld the 
"longstanding legal prinCiple preclUding JUdicial inqulry into the 
motivation or mental processes of legislators in enacting 
legislation." 
County of Los Angeles v. Superior Court (1975) 13 Cal. 
3d 721, 732. 
See also Tenney v. Brandhove (1951) 341 U.S. 367, 71 
S . Ct. 783. 
The fact that under existlng law legislators will not be held to 
answer by executive or jUdiclal agencies for their votes in enacting 
leglslatlon does not, of course, mean that legislative votes based on 
conflicts of interest are permitted. 
It simply means that tne 
11 
All statutory references made are to the Government Code 
unless otherwise noted. 


Stepnen C. Williams 
June 9, 
1~83 
Page 'fwo 
primary responsiolLl~y for enforcement of conflict of interest rules 
in the leglsiatlve arena has oeen left to tne Legislature itself. 
The Legislative Ethics Code (Government Code Section 8920), which 
~rohibits certain conflicts of in~erest by state leglslators, can be 
enforcea by the Legislature's own Joint Ethics Committee. 
There is 
no sanctlon, however, for violations of Sec~ion 87100 by legislators. 
Althougn tne Falr Political Practices Commission has no power to 
impose sanctions on legislators who violate Section 87100, the 
Commission does playa slgnificant role with respect to potential 
conflicts of interest by legislators. 
The Commission issues advice 
or oplnlons to legislators WhO, like Speaker Brown, have questions 
concernlng tne possibility of conflicts of lnterest under the 
Political Reform Act. 
It also, by regulation, defines conflicts of 
interests unaer tne Act. 
Finally, the disclosure provislons of the 
Political Reform Act are designed to prevent legislative conflicts of 
interest. 
It would, of course, be possible, as you suggested, to amend the 
Politlcal Reform Act to give tne Fair Political Practices Commission 
and the courts tne power to enforce Sectlon 87100 against state 
legislators. 
California Constltution, Article 4, Section 5, 
provides: 
The Leglslature shall enact laws to prohibit members 
of the Legislature from engaging in activities or having 
interests Which conflict with the proper discharge of 
their duties and responslbilitiesi provided that tne 
people reserve to tnemselves the power to implement tnis 
requirement pursuant to Section 22 of tnis artlcle. 
(Added Nov. 8, 1977.) 
Your proposal will oe brough~ to the attention of the Commis-
sioners in connection witn their review of posslble legislation for 
the 1984 legislative session. 
If tne Commission does consiaer 
sponsoring legislation to make the sanctions of tne Act available 
against state legislators, you will be given an opportunity to 
present specific comments and suggestions. 
In tne meantime, I hope 
this letter has answered your questions. 
BAl"I! km 
Very truly yo~rs, 
I 
• 
~ '-
/ 
Barbara A. Milman 
Chief, Legal Division 


May 25, 1983 
LAW OFFICES 
Stephan C. Williams 
SUITE 240 
TWIN OAKS BUILDING 
190 N. WIGET LANE 
WALNUT CREEK, CA 94598 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 "K" Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Re: 
The Effect of the Conflicts of Interest Provisions 
TELEPHONE: 939-6822 
AREA CODE: 415 
of the Political Reform Act on Elected State Officers 
Dear Commission Members: 
The recent FPPC bulletin, dated April 29, 1983, contained an 
item under the section entitled "Advice Summary - Conflict of 
Interest" dealing with advice offered to Willie Brown, the Speaker 
of the Assembly. 
Also, if my memory serves me, there T"vas a great 
deal of publicity generated in the media by the fact that Assembly-
man Brown had sought an opinion from the FPPC in respect to the 
potential conflict of interest involving his support of legis-
lation on condominium conversations in the City of San Francisco. 
It appears that the conflict arises as a result of Assemblyman 
Brown's acceptance of a $1,000 gift from a Richard Traweek -
a person T"vho has a substantial interest in the firm which owns 
a large apartment house in the Lake Merced area of San Francisco. 
Also in my mind at the time that I read the latest FPPC bulletin 
was an article I smv in one of the local papers commenting on the 
number of clients of Assemblyman Brown who paid him in fees sums 
ranging above $10,000 over a one year period of time. 
There 
apparently Here 30 such clients. 
I am also reminded of the 
fact that as Speaker of the Assembly, Assemblyman Bro\Vll has 
precious little time to be devoting to a law practice that 
can generate those kinds of fees in the normal course. 
Why Assemblyman BroT"Vll chose to seek the advice of the FPPC on 
his gift from Richard Traweek puzzles me. 
Assemblyman Bro~!'s 
peculiar behavior in respect to the Traweek gift becoriles parti-
cularly perplexing when one reads the conflicts of interest pro-
visions of the Political Reform Act. 
Section 87100 
of the Government Code states as follows: 
"No public official at any level of state or local government 
shall make, participate in making or in any "vay attempt to use 
his official position to influence a governmental decision in 
which he knO\vs or has reason to know he has a financial interest." 
It is clear from reading other sections of the PRA (82048, 
82049) that a "public official" includes state legislators. 


Hay 25, 1983 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Page 2 
We know, therefore, that under the PRA it is unlawful for a 
state legislator to knowingly engage in a conflict of interest. 
However, the sanctions for violating this prohibition do not 
apply to state legislators. 
See Chapter 11 of the PRA, com-
mencing at Government Code Section 91000. 
Paradoxically, 
all members of all local legislative bodies, such as boards 
of supervisors and the city council, can be severely sanctioned 
for violating conflict of interest provisions of the 
PRA - but the same rules do not apply to state legislators. 
This anomaly is noted in your commission's booklet entitled 
"A Guide to Political Reform Act of 1974 - California's Con-
flict of Interest Law for Public Officials." 
In light of the fact that there are no sanctions that can be 
imposed against a state legislator for violating the conflict 
of interest provisions of the PRA, one wonders how the commission 
deals with conflicts of interest on the part of state legislators. 
As the primary enforcer and construer of the provisions of the 
PRA, would it be possible for the commission to sponsor an amend-
ment ~to the act which would close the loophole referred to above? 
.",,/\ 
Very j::ruly ;four s , 
SCW: pm 


June 16, 1983 
Hs. Barbara Hilman 
LAW OFFICES 
Stephan C. Williams 
SUITE 240 
TWIN OAKS BUILDING 
190 N. WI GET LANE ' .. 
WALNUT CREEK. CA 94~.glit 
Fair Political Practices Co~~ission 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804 
Re: 
Your Advice No. A-83-l46 
Dear 1'1s. Milman: 
TELEPHONE: 939-6822 
AREA eOOE: 415 
Thank you for your letter of June 9, 1983, which was in response 
to my inquiry on the non-applicability of imposing sanctions 
against legislators with conflicts of interests. 
I particularly 
appreciate the case citations contained in your second paragraph. 
I suspected that there might have been some constitutional reason 
for the drafters of the act not to include the state legislators 
for the purpose of imposing sanctions. 
I think the important issue raised by my letter is the lack of 
knowledge on the part of the public that the sanction provisions 
of the act do not apply to legislators. 
The non-applicability 
of these sanctions also permits legislators to use the Co~~ission 
by requesting advice letters behind which they can shield them-
selves from donors like Traweek. 
The legislator then gets the 
benefit of good press and also is able to keep the economic 
benefit that he received from the donor. 
I again thank you for your prompt response to my inquiry. 
I shall look forward to the Commission's proposal to change the 
lmv to ,include legislators under Government Code Section 87102. 
Very tn:tlylyour s , 
1 
SCW:pm

sections.question_synthetic: Why do conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act apply to state legislators under Section 87100 but have no enforceable sanctions, and can the FPPC close this gap?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The exemption of legislators from sanctions for Section 87100 violations stems from constitutional principles of separation of powers and legislative immunity. The FPPC has no power to impose sanctions on legislators but provides advisory opinions and disclosure requirements instead. The FPPC will consider bringing the issue to Commissioners for potential legislative amendment in the 1984 session.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a three-part document consisting of: (1) incoming request from Williams to FPPC (5/25/83), (2) FPPC response letter from Barbara Milman (6/9/83), and (3) follow-up correspondence from Williams (6/16/83). The main advice letter (A-83-146) is the 6/9/83 response. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections present; information extracted from body text. Document addresses the anomaly that Section 87100 applies to legislators but Chapter 11 sanctions do not.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Why do conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act apply to state legislators under Section 87100 but have no enforceable sanctions, and can the FPPC close this gap?

CONCLUSION: The exemption of legislators from sanctions for Section 87100 violations stems from constitutional principles of separation of powers and legislative immunity. The FPPC has no power to impose sanctions on legislators but provides advisory opinions and disclosure requirements instead. The FPPC will consider bringing the issue to Commissioners for potential legislative amendment in the 1984 session.

embedding.summary: An attorney inquires why state legislators can violate the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions without facing sanctions, using Assembly Speaker Willie Brown's situation as context. The FPPC responds explaining the constitutional and historical reasons for the legislative exemption and its advisory role.
